title: Lord Howe Island

infobox.value: Lord Howe Island, [Admiralty Group](/wiki/Admiralty_Group "Admiralty Group"), [Blackburn Island](/wiki/Blackburn_Island "Blackburn Island"), Mutton Bird Islands, and [Ball's Pyramid](/wiki/Ball%27s_Pyramid "Ball's Pyramid")

[Unincorporated area](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") Self-governed by the [Lord Howe Island Board](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Board "Lord Howe Island Board")[[1]](#cite_note-RegionalProfile-1)

Part of the [electoral district of Port Macquarie](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Port_Macquarie "Electoral district of Port Macquarie")[[2]](#cite_note-2) Part of the [Division of Sydney](/wiki/Division_of_Sydney "Division of Sydney")[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* 445 * Tourists are restricted to 400 at any time. ([2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"))[[4]](#cite_note-Census2021-4)

[1982](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_by_year_of_inscription#1982_(6th_session) "List of World Heritage Sites by year of inscription") (6th [session](/wiki/World_Heritage_Committee "World Heritage Committee"))
[Asia-Pacific](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Oceania "List of World Heritage Sites in Oceania")

([/haʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); formerly **Lord Howe's Island**) is an irregularly crescent-shaped volcanic remnant in the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") between [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), part of the Australian state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). It lies 600 km (370 mi; 320 nmi) directly east of mainland [Port Macquarie](/wiki/Port_Macquarie "Port Macquarie"), 780 km (480 mi; 420 nmi) northeast of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and about 900 km (560 mi; 490 nmi) southwest of [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"). It is about 10 km (6.2 mi) long and between 0.3 and 2.0 km (0.19 and 1.24 mi) wide with an area of 14.55 km2 (3,600 acres), though just 3.98 km2 (980 acres) of that comprise the low-lying developed part of the island.[[5]](#cite_note-State_of_NSW-5) The island is named after [Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

**Lord Howe Island** ([/haʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); formerly **Lord Howe's Island**) is an irregularly crescent-shaped volcanic remnant in the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") between [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), part of the Australian state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). It lies 600 km (370 mi; 320 nmi) directly east of mainland [Port Macquarie](/wiki/Port_Macquarie "Port Macquarie"), 780 km (480 mi; 420 nmi) northeast of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and about 900 km (560 mi; 490 nmi) southwest of [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"). It is about 10 km (6.2 mi) long and between 0.3 and 2.0 km (0.19 and 1.24 mi) wide with an area of 14.55 km2 (3,600 acres), though just 3.98 km2 (980 acres) of that comprise the low-lying developed part of the island.[[5]](#cite_note-State_of_NSW-5) The island is named after [Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
Along the west coast is a sandy semi-enclosed sheltered [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") lagoon. Most of the population lives in the north, while the south is dominated by forested hills rising to the highest point on the island, Mount Gower (875 m, 2,871 ft).[[7]](#cite_note-Group-7) The Lord Howe Island Group[[7]](#cite_note-Group-7) comprises 28 islands, islets, and rocks. Apart from Lord Howe Island itself, the most notable of these is the volcanic and uninhabited [Ball's Pyramid](/wiki/Ball%27s_Pyramid "Ball's Pyramid") about 23 km (14 mi; 12 nmi) to the southeast of Howe. To the north lies the [Admiralty Group](/wiki/Admiralty_Group "Admiralty Group"), a cluster of seven uninhabited islets.[[8]](#cite_note-Hutton81-8)

The first reported sighting of Lord Howe Island took place on 17 February 1788, when Lieutenant [Henry Lidgbird Ball](/wiki/Henry_Lidgbird_Ball "Henry Lidgbird Ball"), commander of the Armed Tender [HMS *Supply*](/wiki/HMS_Supply_(1759) "HMS Supply (1759)"), was en route from [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") to found a penal settlement on [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island").[[9]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_4-9) On the return journey, Ball sent a party ashore on Lord Howe Island to claim it as a British possession.[[10]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-10) It subsequently became a provisioning port for the whaling industry,[[11]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_28-11) and was permanently settled in June 1834.[[12]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-2-12) When whaling declined, the 1880s saw the beginning of the worldwide export of the endemic [kentia palms](/wiki/Howea_forsteriana "Howea forsteriana"),[[13]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_74-13) which remains a key component of the island's economy. The other continuing industry, tourism, began after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") ended in 1945.

The Lord Howe Island Group is part of the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) and is regarded legally as an [unincorporated area](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") administered by the Lord Howe Island Board,[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) which reports to the [New South Wales Minister for Environment and Heritage](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Minister_for_Environment_and_Heritage "New South Wales Minister for Environment and Heritage").[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) The island's standard [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") is [UTC+10:30](/wiki/UTC%2B10:30 "UTC+10:30"), or [UTC+11](/wiki/UTC%2B11 "UTC+11") when [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") applies.[[15]](#cite_note-info-15) The currency is the [Australian dollar](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar"). Commuter airlines provide flights to [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport"), [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Airport "Gold Coast Airport"), [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_Airport,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle Airport, New South Wales"), and [Port Macquarie](/wiki/Port_Macquarie_Airport "Port Macquarie Airport").

[UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") records the Lord Howe Island Group as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") of global natural significance.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Most of the island is virtually untouched [forest](/wiki/Forest "Forest"), with many of the plants and animals found nowhere else in the world. Other natural attractions include the diversity of the landscapes, the variety of [upper mantle](/wiki/Upper_mantle "Upper mantle") and [oceanic basalts](/wiki/Ocean_island_basalt "Ocean island basalt"), the world's southernmost barrier [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef"), nesting [seabirds](/wiki/Seabird "Seabird"), and the rich historical and cultural heritage.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19865–6-17) The Lord Howe Island Act 1981 established a "Permanent Park Preserve" (covering about 70% of the island).[[15]](#cite_note-info-15) The island was added to the [Australian National Heritage List](/wiki/Australian_National_Heritage_List "Australian National Heritage List") on 21 May 2007[[18]](#cite_note-ANHL-18) and the [New South Wales State Heritage Register](/wiki/New_South_Wales_State_Heritage_Register "New South Wales State Heritage Register") on 2 April 1999.[[19]](#cite_note-nswshr-970-19) The surrounding waters are a protected region designated the [Lord Howe Island Marine Park](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Marine_Park "Lord Howe Island Marine Park").[[20]](#cite_note-MarinePark-20)

## Bioregion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bioregion")]

Lord Howe Island is part of the [IBRA](/wiki/IBRA "IBRA") region [Pacific Subtropical Islands](/wiki/Pacific_Subtropical_Islands "Pacific Subtropical Islands") and is subregion PSI01 with an area of 1,909 ha (4,720 acres).[[21]](#cite_note-IBRA7-21) In the WWF [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") system, Lord Howe Island constitutes the entirety of the 'Lord Howe Island subtropical forests' ecoregion (WWF ID#AA0109). This ecoregion is in the [Australasian realm](/wiki/Australasian_realm "Australasian realm"), and the [tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests](/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_moist_broadleaf_forests "Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests") [biome](/wiki/Biome "Biome"). The WWF ecoregion has an area of 14 km2 (5½ sq. mi).[[22]](#cite_note-ecomap-22)[[23]](#cite_note-dopa-23)[[24]](#cite_note-eoe-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Before European discovery and settlement, Lord Howe Island apparently was uninhabited, and unknown to [Polynesian peoples](/wiki/Polynesian_peoples "Polynesian peoples") of the [South Pacific](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols20065-25) No evidence to suggest prehistoric human activity has ever been found on Lord Howe Island, even after an extensive archaeological investigation in 1996.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In *Australian Archaeology* Atholl Anderson concluded in 2003 that, "the case for no pre-European settlement on Lord Howe Island is now more compelling than before" and that "the absence of pre-European settlement on Lord Howe Island is not easily explained. At 16 km2 (6¼ sq. mi) it is twice the size of [Pitcairn Island](/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands "Pitcairn Islands") and half the size of [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"), two other remote subtropical islands that were inhabited prehistorically, and it had a [biota](/wiki/Biome "Biome") very similar to that of Norfolk Island."[[27]](#cite_note-aa-27)

### 1788–1834: First European visits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1788–1834: First European visits")]

[![Picture a silhouette profile of Lieutenant Lidgbird Ball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/HenryLidgbirdBall.jpg/250px-HenryLidgbirdBall.jpg)](/wiki/File:HenryLidgbirdBall.jpg)

A [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette "Silhouette") of Lieutenant [Henry Lidgbird Ball](/wiki/Henry_Lidgbird_Ball "Henry Lidgbird Ball"), the commander of HMS *Supply*

The first reported sighting of Lord Howe Island was on 17 February 1788 by Lieutenant [Henry Lidgbird Ball](/wiki/Henry_Lidgbird_Ball "Henry Lidgbird Ball"), commander of the Armed Tender [HMS *Supply*](/wiki/HMAT_Supply_(1759) "HMAT Supply (1759)") (the oldest and smallest of the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") ships), which was on its way from Botany Bay with a cargo of nine male and six female convicts to found a [penal settlement](/wiki/Penal_settlement "Penal settlement") on [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island").[[9]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_4-9) On the return journey of 13 March 1788, Ball observed [Ball's Pyramid](/wiki/Ball%27s_Pyramid "Ball's Pyramid") and sent a party ashore on Lord Howe Island to claim it as a British possession.[[10]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-10) Numerous turtles and tame birds were captured and returned to Sydney.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197210-28) Ball named [Mount Lidgbird](/wiki/Mount_Lidgbird "Mount Lidgbird") and Ball's Pyramid after himself and the main island after [Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe"), who was [First Lord of the Admiralty](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Admiralty "First Lord of the Admiralty") at the time.[[10]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-10)

A drawing made in May 1788 by [Arthur Bowes Smyth](/wiki/Arthur_Bowes_Smyth "Arthur Bowes Smyth") who was the surgeon aboard [*Lady Penrhyn*](/wiki/Lady_Penrhyn_(1786_ship) "Lady Penrhyn (1786 ship)"), a ship of the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") heading for China on its return trip to England.
Many names on the island date from this time, and also from May of the same year, when four ships of the First Fleet, [HMS *Supply*](/wiki/HMAT_Supply_(1759) "HMAT Supply (1759)"), [*Charlotte*](/wiki/Charlotte_(1784_ship) "Charlotte (1784 ship)"), *[Lady Penrhyn](/wiki/Lady_Penrhyn "Lady Penrhyn")* and [*Scarborough*](/wiki/Scarborough_(1782_ship) "Scarborough (1782 ship)"), visited it. Much of the plant and animal life was first recorded in the journals and diaries of visitors such as [David Blackburn](/wiki/David_Blackburn_(Royal_Navy_officer) "David Blackburn (Royal Navy officer)"), Master of *Supply*, and [Arthur Bowes Smyth](/wiki/Arthur_Bowes_Smyth "Arthur Bowes Smyth"),[[29]](#cite_note-Catalogue_Bowes-Smyth-29) surgeon of *Lady Penrhyn*.

Smyth was in Sydney when *Supply* returned from the first voyage to Norfolk Island. His journal entry for 19 March 1788 noted that:

> "...the *Supply*, in her return, landed at the island she [discovered] in going out, and all were very agreeably surprised to find great numbers of a fine turtle on the beach and, on the land amongst the trees, great numbers of fowls very like a guinea hen, and another species of fowl not unlike the landrail in England, and all so perfectly tame that you could frequently take hold of them with your hands but could, at all times, knock down as many as you thought proper, with a short stick. Inside the reef also there were fish innumerable, which were so easily taken with a hook and line as to be able to catch a boat full in a short time. She brought thirteen large turtles to Port Jackson and many were distributed among the camp and fleet."[[29]](#cite_note-Catalogue_Bowes-Smyth-29)

[![Original drawing of the extinct white gallinule by Arthur Bowes Smyth, surgeon aboard First Fleet ship Lady Penrhyn](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Representation_of_a_Bird_of_the_Coot_kind%2C_found_at_Lord_Howe_Island_A604008h.jpg/500px-Representation_of_a_Bird_of_the_Coot_kind%2C_found_at_Lord_Howe_Island_A604008h.jpg)](/wiki/File:Representation_of_a_Bird_of_the_Coot_kind,_found_at_Lord_Howe_Island_A604008h.jpg)

The extinct white swamphen, 1788 watercolour by [Arthur Bowes Smyth](/wiki/Arthur_Bowes_Smyth "Arthur Bowes Smyth")

Watercolour sketches of native birds including the [Lord Howe woodhen](/wiki/Lord_Howe_woodhen "Lord Howe woodhen"), [white swamphen](/wiki/White_swamphen "White swamphen") and [Lord Howe pigeon](/wiki/Lord_Howe_pigeon "Lord Howe pigeon") were made by artists including [George Raper](/wiki/George_Raper "George Raper") and [John Hunter](/wiki/John_Hunter_(Royal_Navy_officer) "John Hunter (Royal Navy officer)"). As the latter two birds were soon hunted to extinction, these paintings are their only remaining pictorial record.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-library-31) Over the next three years, *Supply* returned to the island several times in search of turtles, and the island was also visited by ships of the [Second](/wiki/Second_Fleet_(Australia) "Second Fleet (Australia)") and [Third Fleets](/wiki/Third_Fleet_(Australia) "Third Fleet (Australia)").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols20068-32) Between 1789 and 1791, the Pacific [whale industry](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling") was born with British and American whaling ships chasing [sperm whales](/wiki/Sperm_whale "Sperm whale") along the equator to the [Gilbert and Ellice archipelago](/wiki/Gilbert_and_Ellice_Islands "Gilbert and Ellice Islands"), then south into Australian and New Zealand waters.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200621-33) The American fleet numbered 675 ships and Lord Howe was located in a region known as the Middle Ground noted for sperm whales and [southern right whales](/wiki/Southern_right_whale "Southern right whale").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200623–24-34)

The island was subsequently visited by many governments and whaling ships sailing between New South Wales and Norfolk Island and across the Pacific, including many from the American whaling fleet, so its reputation as a provisioning port preceded settlement,[[11]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_28-11) with some ships leaving goats and pigs on the island as food for future visitors. Between July and October 1791, the Third Fleet ships arrived at Sydney and within days, the deck work was being reconstructed for a future in the lucrative whaling industry. [Whale oil](/wiki/Whale_oil "Whale oil") was to become Australia's most profitable export until the 1830s, and the whaling industry shaped Lord Howe Island's early history.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200617-35)

### 1834–1841: Settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1834–1841: Settlement")]

[![Photograph of the main room of the Lord Howe Island Museum with diversity of exhibits](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Lord_Howe_Island_maritime_museum.jpg/250px-Lord_Howe_Island_maritime_museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Howe_Island_maritime_museum.jpg)

Lord Howe Island Maritime Museum and Information Centre

Permanent settlement on Lord Howe was established in [June 1834](https://www.flickr.com/photos/blackdiamondimages/14151018510), when the British whaling [barque](/wiki/Barque "Barque") *Caroline*, sailing from [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and commanded by Captain John Blinkenthorpe, landed at what is now known as Blinky Beach. They left three men, George Ashdown, James Bishop, and Chapman, who was employed by a Sydney whaling firm to establish a supply station. The men were initially to provide meat by fishing and by raising pigs and goats from feral stock. They landed with (or acquired from a visiting ship) their [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") wives and two Māori boys. Huts were built in an area now known as Old Settlement, which had a supply of fresh water, and a garden was established west of Blinky Beach.[[12]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-2-12)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200632-36)

The settlers [bartered](/wiki/Barter "Barter") their stores of water, wood, vegetables, meat, fish, and bird feathers for clothes, tea, sugar, tools, tobacco, and other commodities not available on the island, but it was the whalers' valuation that had to be accepted.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197238-37)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197223-38) These first settlers eventually left the island when they were bought out for [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")350 in September 1841 by businessmen Owen Poole and Richard Dawson (later joined by John Foulis), whose employees and others then settled on the island.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197226–30-39)

### 1842–1860: Trading provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1842–1860: Trading provisions")]

The new business was advertised and ships trading between Sydney and the [New Hebrides](/wiki/New_Hebrides "New Hebrides") ([Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu")) would also be put into the island. *Rover's Bride*, a small cutter, became the first regular trading vessel.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200634-40) Between 1839 and 1859, five to 12 ships made landfall each year, occasionally closer to 20, with seven or eight at a time laying off the reef.[[41]](#cite_note-nich-41) In 1842 and 1844, the first children were born on the island. Then in 1847, Poole, Dawson, and Foulis, bitter at failing to obtain a land lease from the New South Wales government, abandoned the settlement although three of their employees remained.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197230-42) One family, the Andrews, after finding some [onions](/wiki/Onion "Onion") on the beach in 1848, cultivated them as the "Lord Howe red onion", which was popular in the Southern Hemisphere for about 30 years until the crop was attacked by [smut disease](/wiki/Smut_(fungus) "Smut (fungus)").[[43]](#cite_note-Smut-43)

[![Etching of HMS Herald from the London Illustrated News with the steamship Torch in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/HMS_Herald.jpg/250px-HMS_Herald.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Herald.jpg)

"Expedition to the South Seas: HMS *Herald* and steamship tender *Torch*" *[The Illustrated London News](/wiki/The_Illustrated_London_News "The Illustrated London News")*, 15 May 1852

In 1849, just 11 people were living on the island, but soon the island farms expanded.[[43]](#cite_note-Smut-43) In the 1850s, [gold was discovered](/wiki/Australian_gold_rushes "Australian gold rushes") on mainland Australia, where crews would abandon their ships, preferring to dig for gold than risk their lives at sea. As a consequence, many vessels avoided the mainland and Lord Howe Island experienced an increasing trade, which peaked between 1855 and 1857.[[44]](#cite_note-nich2-44) In 1851, about 16 people were living on the island.[[12]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-2-12) Vegetable crops now included potatoes, carrots, maize, pumpkin, taro, watermelon, and even grapes, passionfruit, and coffee.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200632-36) Between 1851 and 1853, several aborted proposals were made by the NSW government to establish a penal settlement on the island.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200638–40-45)

From 1851 to 1854, [Henry Denham](/wiki/Henry_Denham "Henry Denham") captain of [HMS *Herald*](/wiki/HMS_Herald_(1824) "HMS Herald (1824)"), which was on a [scientific expedition](/wiki/European_and_American_voyages_of_scientific_exploration "European and American voyages of scientific exploration") to the southwest Pacific (1852–1856), completed the island's first [hydrographic survey](/wiki/Hydrographic_survey "Hydrographic survey"). On board were three Scottish biologists, [William Milne](/wiki/William_Grant_Milne "William Grant Milne") (a [gardener-botanist](/wiki/List_of_gardener-botanist_explorers_of_the_Enlightenment "List of gardener-botanist explorers of the Enlightenment") from the [Edinburgh Botanic Garden](/wiki/Edinburgh_Botanic_Garden "Edinburgh Botanic Garden")), [John MacGillivray](/wiki/John_MacGillivray "John MacGillivray") (naturalist) who collected fish and plant specimens, and assistant surgeon and zoologist [Denis Macdonald](/wiki/John_Denis_Macdonald "John Denis Macdonald"). Together, these men established much basic information on the island's geology, flora, and fauna.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197230–31-46)

Around 1853, a further three settlers arrived on the American whaling barque *Belle*, captained by Ichabod Handy.[[47]](#cite_note-47) George Campbell (who died in 1856) and Jack Brian (who left the island in 1854) arrived, and the third, Nathan Thompson, brought three women (called Botanga, Bogoroo, and a girl named Bogue) from the [Gilbert Islands](/wiki/Gilbert_Islands "Gilbert Islands"). When his first wife Botanga died, he then married Bogue. Thompson was the first resident to build a substantial house in the 1860s from mainland cedar washed up on the beach.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19862,_44-48) Most of the residents with island ancestors have blood relations or are connected by marriage to Thompson and his second wife Bogue.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197233-49)

In 1855, the island was officially designated as part of New South Wales by the Constitution Act.[[50]](#cite_note-Constitution_Act-50)

### 1861–1890: Scientific expeditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1861–1890: Scientific expeditions")]

From the early 1860s, whaling declined rapidly with the increasing use of petroleum, the onset of the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush"), and the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")—with unfortunate consequences for the people of the island. To explore alternative means of income, Thompson, in 1867, purchased *Sylph*, which was the first local vessel to trade with Sydney (mainly pigs and onions). It anchored in deep water at what is now Sylph's Hole off Old Settlement Beach but was eventually tragically lost at sea in 1873, which added to the woes of the island at that time.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200658–59-51)

In 1869, the island was visited by magistrate P. Cloete aboard *Thetis* investigating a possible murder. He was accompanied by [Charles Moore](/wiki/Charles_Moore_(botanist) "Charles Moore (botanist)"), director of the [Botanic Gardens in Sydney](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Garden,_Sydney "Royal Botanic Garden, Sydney"), and his assistant William Carron, who forwarded plant specimens to [Ferdinand von Mueller](/wiki/Ferdinand_von_Mueller "Ferdinand von Mueller") at the botanic gardens in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), who by 1875, had catalogued and published 195 species.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMueller187576–79-52) Also on the ship was William Fitzgerald, a surveyor, and Mr. Masters from the [Australian Museum](/wiki/Australian_Museum "Australian Museum"). Together, they surveyed the island with the findings published in 1870 when the population was listed as 35 people, their 13 houses built of split [palm](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut") [battens](/wiki/Batten "Batten") [thatched](/wiki/Thatched "Thatched") on the roof and sides with palm leaves.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Around this time, a downturn of trade began with the demise of the whaling industry, and sometimes six to 12 months passed without a vessel calling. The older families lost interest in market gardening with the provisions rotting in the storehouses.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197240-54)

From 1860 to 1872, 43 ships had collected provisions, but from 1873 to 1887, fewer than a dozen had done so.[[44]](#cite_note-nich2-44) This prompted some activity from the mainland. In 1876, a government report on the island was submitted by surveyor William Fitzgerald based on a visit in the same year. He suggested that coffee be grown, but the [kentia palm](/wiki/Kentia_palm "Kentia palm") was already catching world attention.[[13]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_74-13) In 1878, the island was declared a forest reserve and Captain Richard Armstrong became the first resident government administrator. He encouraged schools, tree-planting, and the palm trade, dynamited the north passage to the lagoon, and built roads. He also managed to upset the residents, and parliamentarian [Bowie Wilson](/wiki/Bowie_Wilson "Bowie Wilson") was sent from the mainland in April 1882 to investigate the situation.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200676–77,_82–3-55) With Wilson was a team of scientists who included H. Wilkinson from the Mines Department, W. Condor from the Survey Department, J. Duff from the Sydney Botanical Gardens, and A. Morton from the Australian Museum. J. Sharkey from the Government Printing Office took the earliest known photographs of the island and its residents. A full account of the island appeared in the report from this visit,[[56]](#cite_note-56) which recommended that Armstrong be replaced. Meanwhile, the population had increased considerably and included 29 children; the report recommended that a schoolmaster be appointed.[[57]](#cite_note-Green_1994_15-57) This study sealed a lasting relationship with three scientific organisations, the Australian Museum, Sydney Royal Botanic Gardens, and [Kew Royal Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Kew_Royal_Botanic_Gardens "Kew Royal Botanic Gardens").

### 1890–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1890–1999")]

In 1883, the company [Burns Philp](/wiki/Burns_Philp "Burns Philp") started a regular shipping service and the number of tourists gradually increased. By 1932, with the regular tourist run of SS *Morinda*, tourism became the second-largest source of external income after palm sales to Europe.[[12]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-2-12) *Morinda* was replaced by [*Makambo*](/wiki/SS_Makambo "SS Makambo") in 1932, and she in turn by other vessels. The service continues into the present day with the fortnightly *Island Trader* service from Port Macquarie.[[58]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_139-58)

The palm trade began in the 1880s when the lowland kentia palm (*[Howea forsteriana](/wiki/Howea_forsteriana "Howea forsteriana")*) was first exported to Britain, Europe, and America, but the trade was only placed on a firm financial footing when the Lord Howe Island Kentia Palm Nursery was formed in 1906 (see below).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/LordHoweIsland_NorthBay_Reef_35.jpg/250px-LordHoweIsland_NorthBay_Reef_35.jpg)](/wiki/File:LordHoweIsland_NorthBay_Reef_35.jpg)

The 1965 wreck of *The Favourite* in North Bay is a popular site

The first plane to appear on the island was in 1931, when [Francis Chichester](/wiki/Francis_Chichester "Francis Chichester") alighted on the lagoon in a [de Havilland Gipsy Moth](/wiki/De_Havilland_Gipsy_Moth "De Havilland Gipsy Moth") converted into a [floatplane](/wiki/Floatplane "Floatplane"). It was damaged there in an overnight storm, but repaired with the assistance of islanders and then took off successfully nine weeks later for a flight to Sydney.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006132-59) After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in 1947, tourists arrived on [Catalina](/wiki/Consolidated_PBY_Catalina "Consolidated PBY Catalina") and then four-engined [Sandringham](/wiki/Short_Sandringham "Short Sandringham") flying boats of [Ansett Flying Boat Services](/wiki/Ansett_Australia#Towards_a_duopoly "Ansett Australia") operating out of [Rose Bay](/wiki/Rose_Bay,_New_South_Wales "Rose Bay, New South Wales"), Sydney, and landing on the lagoon, the journey taking about 3½ hours. When [Lord Howe Island Airport](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Airport "Lord Howe Island Airport") was completed in 1974, the seaplanes were eventually replaced with [QantasLink](/wiki/QantasLink "QantasLink") twin-engined turboprop [Dash 8–200](/wiki/Bombardier_Dash_8 "Bombardier Dash 8") aircraft.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19863–4-60)

In 1990, [Lance Knight](/w/index.php?title=Lance_Knight&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lance Knight (page does not exist)") the master of the island's supply ship *Sitka* called a meeting in the town hall to propose the formation of an island owned shipping service.
Lance set off around the world to find a ship and whilst in South-East Asia made one of the most well-known surf discoveries of all time at [Lance's Right](/wiki/Lance%27s_Right "Lance's Right") on the island of Sipora in the [Mentawai Islands](/wiki/Mentawai_Islands "Mentawai Islands") of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").
Lance eventually returned to the island with its now iconic ship, MV *Island Trader*.
[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Island_Trader.jpg/250px-Island_Trader.jpg)](/wiki/File:Island_Trader.jpg)

*Island Trader*, a fortnightly feature to the island, with the mountains in the background.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2002, the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") destroyer [HMS *Nottingham*](/wiki/HMS_Nottingham_(D91) "HMS Nottingham (D91)") struck [Wolf Rock](/wiki/Wolf_Rock,_Lord_Howe_Island "Wolf Rock, Lord Howe Island"), a reef at Lord Howe Island, and almost sank.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In recent times, tourism has increased and the government of New South Wales has been increasingly involved with issues of conservation.[[12]](#cite_note-Hutton_1986-2-12)

On 17 October 2011, the supply ship MV *Island Trader*, carrying 20 tonnes of fuel, ran aground in the lagoon. The ship refloated at high tide with no loss of crew or cargo.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The 2016 film *[The Shallows](/wiki/The_Shallows_(film) "The Shallows (film)")* starring [Blake Lively](/wiki/Blake_Lively "Blake Lively") was largely filmed on the island.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

During the [COVID-19 pandemic in New South Wales](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_South_Wales "COVID-19 pandemic in New South Wales"), a public health order was issued on 22 March 2020 that declared Lord Howe Island a public risk area and directed restricted access. As of that date there were no known cases of COVID-19 on the Island.[[65]](#cite_note-lhi-info-covid-order-65)[[66]](#cite_note-nsw-health-island-order-mar'20-66)

One of the most contentious issues amongst islanders in the 21st century is what to do about the [rodent](/wiki/Rodent "Rodent") situation. Rodents have only been on the island since the [SS *Makambo*](/wiki/SS_Makambo "SS Makambo") ran aground in 1918, and have wiped out several endemic bird species and were thought to have done the same to the [Lord Howe Island stick insect](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect "Lord Howe Island stick insect"). A plan in 2016 was made to drop 42 tonnes of rat bait across the island, but the community was heavily divided.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The island was due to be declared rodent-free in October 2021, two years after the last live rat was found, but a living male and pregnant female were discovered in April 2021.[[68]](#cite_note-68) As of October 2023, the island is considered rat free.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The eradication, contrary to many community reservations, has seen birds, insects and plants flourish at levels not seen in decades.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As at the 2016 census, the resident population was 382 people,[[71]](#cite_note-71) and the number of tourists was not allowed to exceed 400.[[72]](#cite_note-travel-72) Early settlers were European and American whalers and many of their offspring have remained on the island for more than six generations. Residents now work in the kentia palm industry, tourism, retail, some fishing, and farming. In 1876 on Sundays, games and labour were suspended, but no religious services were held.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197240-54) Nowadays, the area known locally as Church Paddock has [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), and [Adventist](/wiki/Adventist "Adventist") churches, the religious affiliations on the island being 30% Anglican, 22% no religion, 18% Catholic and 12% Seventh Day Adventist.[[73]](#cite_note-census2011-73) The ratio of the sexes is roughly equal, with 47% of the population in the age group 25–54 and 92% holding [Australian citizenship](/wiki/Australian_citizenship "Australian citizenship").[[73]](#cite_note-census2011-73)

## Governance and land tenure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Governance and land tenure")]

See also: [Lord Howe Island Board](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Board "Lord Howe Island Board")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/05/Flag_of_Lord_Howe_Island%2C_Australia.svg/250px-Flag_of_Lord_Howe_Island%2C_Australia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Lord_Howe_Island,_Australia.svg)

Flag

Official control of Lord Howe Island lay initially with the [British Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") until it passed to New South Wales in 1855,[[50]](#cite_note-Constitution_Act-50)[[74]](#cite_note-74) although, until at least 1876, the islanders lived in "a relatively harmonious and self-regulating community".[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006106-75) In 1878, Richard Armstrong was appointed administrator when the NSW Parliament declared the island a forest reserve, but as a result of ill feeling, and an enquiry, he was eventually removed from office on 31 May 1882 (he returned later that year though to view the [transit of Venus](/wiki/Transit_of_Venus "Transit of Venus") from present-day Transit Hill).[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200682–83-76) After his removal, the island was administered by four successive magistrates until 1913, when a Sydney-based board was formed; in 1948, a resident superintendent was appointed.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200687,_106-77) In 1913, the three-man Lord Howe Island Board of Control was established, mostly to regulate the [palm seed](/wiki/Palm_seed "Palm seed") industry, but also administering the affairs of the island from Sydney until the present Lord Howe Island Board was set up in 1954.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006107–108-78)

The [Lord Howe Island Board](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Board "Lord Howe Island Board") is an NSW Statutory Authority established under the Lord Howe Island Act 1953, to administer the island as part of the state of New South Wales. It reports directly to the state's [Minister for Environment and Heritage](/wiki/Robyn_Parker "Robyn Parker") and is responsible for the island's care, control, and management. Its duties include the protection of [World Heritage](/wiki/World_Heritage "World Heritage") values; the control of development; the administration of Crown Land, including the island's protected area; provision of community services and infrastructure; and regulating [sustainable tourism](/wiki/Sustainable_tourism "Sustainable tourism").[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) In 1981, the Lord Howe Island Amendment Act gave islanders the administrative power of three members on a five-member board.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19863-79) The board also manages the Lord Howe Island kentia palm nursery, which together with tourism, provides the island's only source of external income.[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) Under an amendment bill in 2004, the board now comprises seven members, four of whom are elected from the islander community,[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) thus giving about 350 permanent residents a high level of autonomy. The remaining three members are appointed by the minister to represent the interests of business, tourism, and conservation.[[14]](#cite_note-Board-14) The full board meets on the island every three months, while the day-to-day affairs of the island are managed by the board's administration, with a permanent staff that had increased to 22 people by 1988.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

[Land tenure](/wiki/Land_tenure "Land tenure") has been an issue since the first settlement, as island residents repeatedly requested [freehold title](/wiki/Freehold_title "Freehold title") or an [absolute gift](/wiki/Land_grant "Land grant") of cultivated land.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006110–115-81) Original settlers were [squatters](/wiki/Squatters "Squatters"). The granting of a 100-acre (40 ha) lease to Richard Armstrong in 1878 drew complaints, and a few short-term leases ("permissive occupancies") were granted.[[82]](#cite_note-hundred-82) In 1913, with the appointment of a board of control, permissive occupancies were revoked and the board itself given permissive occupancy of the island.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006111-83) Then the Lord Howe Island Act 1953 made all land the property of the Crown. Direct descendants of islanders with permissive occupancies in 1913 were granted perpetual leases on blocks up to five acres (2.0 ha) for residential purposes. Short-term special leases were granted for larger areas used for agriculture, so in 1955, 55 perpetual leases and 43 special leases were granted.[[84]](#cite_note-Nichols114-84) The 1981 amendment to the act extended political and land rights to all residents of 10 years or more.[[84]](#cite_note-Nichols114-84) An active debate exists concerning the proportion of residents with tenure and the degree of influence on the board of resident islanders about long-term planning for visitors, and issues relating to the environment, amenities, and global heritage.[[82]](#cite_note-hundred-82)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Kentia palm industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Kentia palm industry")]

The first exporter of [palm seeds](/wiki/Palm_seed "Palm seed") was Ned King, a mountain guide for the Fitzgerald surveys of 1869 and 1876, who sent seed to the Sydney Botanic Gardens. Overseas trade began in the 1880s, when one of the four palms endemic to the island, the kentia palm (*[Howea forsteriana](/wiki/Howea_forsteriana "Howea forsteriana")*), which grows naturally in the lowlands, was found to be ideally suited to the fashionable conservatories of the well-to-do in Britain, Europe, and the United States.[[15]](#cite_note-info-15)[[57]](#cite_note-Green_1994_15-57) The assistance of mainland magistrate, Frank Farnell, was needed to put the business on a sound commercial footing when in 1906, he became company director of the Lord Howe Island Kentia Palm Nursery, whose shareholders included 21 islanders and a Sydney-based seed company. However, the formation of the Lord Howe Island Board of Control was needed in 1913 to resolve outstanding issues.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200698–105-106)

The kentia palm (known locally as the thatch palm, as it was used to roof the houses of the early settlers) is popular worldwide as a decorative palm that grows well both outdoors (in sufficiently warm climates) and indoors; the mild climate of the island has led to the evolution of a palm that can tolerate low light, a dry atmosphere, and lowish temperatures — ideal for indoor conditions,[[15]](#cite_note-info-15) particularly in higher-end venues such as hotel lobbies, galleries and large foyers where their high price (thanks to their relative rarity) is no disincentive to their use. Up until the 1980s, the palms were only sold as seed but from then onwards only as high-quality seedlings. The nursery received certification in 1997 for its high-quality management complying with the requirements of [Australian Standard](/wiki/Australian_Standard "Australian Standard") AS/NZS ISO 9002.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

Seed is gathered from both natural forests and plantations, most collectors being descendants of the original settlers. The seed is then germinated in soilless media and sealed from the atmosphere to prevent contamination. After testing, seedlings are picked, washed (bare-rooted), sanitized, and certified, then packed and sealed into insulated containers for export. Nursery profits, in turn, are used in projects that enhance the island's ecosystems. The nursery plans to expand the business to include the curly palm (*Howea belmoreana*) and other native plants of special interest.[[15]](#cite_note-info-15)

By the late 1980s, annual exports had begun to provide a revenue of over [A$](/wiki/A$ "A$")2 million, constituting the only major industry on the island apart from tourism.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Shopping_Centre_Ned%27s_Beach_Road.jpg/250px-Shopping_Centre_Ned%27s_Beach_Road.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shopping_Centre_Ned%27s_Beach_Road.jpg)

Shopping centre on Ned's Beach Road

Lord Howe Island is known for its geology, birds, plants, and marine life. Popular tourist activities include [scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving"), birdwatching, [snorkelling](/wiki/Snorkelling "Snorkelling"), [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing"), [kayaking](/wiki/Kayaking "Kayaking"), and [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing").[[109]](#cite_note-109) To relieve pressure on the small island environment, only 400 tourists are permitted at any one time.[[72]](#cite_note-travel-72) The island is reached by plane from [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport") to [Lord Howe Island Airport](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Airport "Lord Howe Island Airport") in less than two hours.[[72]](#cite_note-travel-72) The Permanent Park Preserve declared in 1981 has similar management guidelines to a [national park](/wiki/National_park "National park").[[110]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_117-110)

#### Facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Facilities")]

With fewer than 800 people on the island at any time, facilities are limited; they include a bakery, butcher, general store, liquor store, restaurants, post office, museum, and information centre, a police officer, a ranger, and an ATM at the bowling club. Stores are shipped to the island fortnightly by *Island Trader* from Port Macquarie.[[58]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_139-58) The island has a small four-bed hospital and dispensary.[[111]](#cite_note-GettingThere-111) A small botanic garden displays labelled local plants in its grounds. Most electricity is supplied by 1.3 MW solar power and 3.7 MWh [battery](/wiki/Battery_storage_power_station "Battery storage power station")[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) at 240 volts [AC](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current"), complemented by [diesel generators](/wiki/Diesel_generator "Diesel generator").[[111]](#cite_note-GettingThere-111) No public transport or mobile phone coverage is available, but public telephones, fax facilities, and internet access are,[[111]](#cite_note-GettingThere-111) as well as a local radio station and newsletter, *The Signal*.

Tourist accommodation ranges from luxury lodges to apartments and villa units.[[114]](#cite_note-Accommodation-114) The currency is the Australian dollar, and there are two banks.[[111]](#cite_note-GettingThere-111) No camping facilities are on the island and remote-area camping is not permitted.[[114]](#cite_note-Accommodation-114) To protect the fragile environment of Ball's Pyramid (which carries the last remaining wild population of the endangered [Lord Howe Island stick insect](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect "Lord Howe Island stick insect")), recreational climbing there is prohibited. No pets are allowed without permission from the board. Islanders use tanked [rainwater](/wiki/Rain "Rain"), supplemented by bore water for showers and washing clothes.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

#### Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Lord_Howe_Golf_Course_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Lord_Howe_Golf_Course_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Howe_Golf_Course_-_panoramio.jpg)

Lord Howe Golf Course

[![View of Little Island at the foot of sheer face of Mount Lidgbird](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Bushwalkig_to_Little_island_along_the_Mt_Lidgbird_track_Lord_Howe_Island_8June2011.jpg/250px-Bushwalkig_to_Little_island_along_the_Mt_Lidgbird_track_Lord_Howe_Island_8June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bushwalkig_to_Little_island_along_the_Mt_Lidgbird_track_Lord_Howe_Island_8June2011.jpg)

[Bushwalking](/wiki/Bushwalking "Bushwalking") to Little Island (bottom right) – Mount Lidgbird track

As distances to sites of interest are short, [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") is the main means of transport on the island. Tourist activities include [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") (9-hole), [lawn bowls](/wiki/Lawn_bowls "Lawn bowls"), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing") (including deep-sea game fishing), [yachting](/wiki/Yachting "Yachting"), [windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing"), [kitesurfing](/wiki/Kitesurfing "Kitesurfing"), [kayaking](/wiki/Kayaking "Kayaking"), and boat trips (including [glass-bottom](/wiki/Glass-bottom_boat "Glass-bottom boat") tours of the lagoon).[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006166–171-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) Swimming, [snorkelling](/wiki/Snorkelling "Snorkelling"), and [scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving") are also popular in the lagoon, as well as off Tenth of June Island, a small rocky outcrop in the Admiralty group where an [underwater plateau](/wiki/Underwater_plateau "Underwater plateau") drops 36 m (118 ft) to reveal extensive [gorgonia](/wiki/Gorgonia "Gorgonia") and [black corals](/wiki/Black_coral "Black coral") growing on the vertical walls. Other diving sites are found off Ball's Pyramid, 26 km (16 mi) away, where trenches, caves, and volcanic drop-offs occur.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Bushwalking, natural history tours, talks, and guided walks take place along the many tracks, the most challenging being the eight-hour guided hike to the top of Mount Gower.[[119]](#cite_note-119) The island has 11 beaches, and hand-feeding the 1 m-long (3 ft) [kingfish](/wiki/Yellowtail_kingfish "Yellowtail kingfish") (*Seriola lalandi*) and large [wrasse](/wiki/Wrasse "Wrasse") at Ned's Beach is very popular.[[120]](#cite_note-120) Walking tracks cover the island with difficulty graded from 1–5, they include—in the north: Transit Hill 2-hour return, 2 km (1.2 mi); Clear Place, 1– to 2-hour return; Stevens Reserve; North Bay, 4-hour return, 4 km (2.5 mi); Mount Eliza; Old Gulch, 20-minute return, 300 m (330 yd); Malabar Hill and Kims Lookout, 3 or 5-hour return, 7 km (4.3 mi) and—in the south: Goat House Cave, 5-hour return, 6 km (3.7 mi); Mount Gower, 8-hour return, 14 km (8.7 mi); Rocky Run and Boat Harbour; Intermediate Hill, 45-minute return, 1 km (0.62 mi); and Little Island, 40-minute return, 3 km (1.9 mi). Recreational climbers must obtain permission from the Lord Howe Island Board.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Ball%27s_Pyramid2.jpg/250px-Ball%27s_Pyramid2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ball%27s_Pyramid2.jpg)

[Ball's Pyramid](/wiki/Ball%27s_Pyramid "Ball's Pyramid"), 23 km (14 mi) southeast of the main island

Lord Howe Island is an irregularly crescent-shaped volcanic remnant in the southwest Pacific Ocean. Lying in the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") between [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), the island is 600 km (370 mi) east of mainland [Port Macquarie](/wiki/Port_Macquarie "Port Macquarie"), 702 km (436 mi) northeast of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and about 772 km (480 mi) from Norfolk Island to its northeast.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERabone197218-123) The island is about 10 km (6.2 mi) long and between 0.3 and 2.0 km (0.19 and 1.24 mi) wide with an area of 14.55 km2 (5.62 sq mi). Along the west coast is a semienclosed, sheltered [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") [lagoon](/wiki/Lagoon "Lagoon") with white sand, the most accessible of the island's 11 beaches.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Lord_Howe_Island_from_North.jpg/250px-Lord_Howe_Island_from_North.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Howe_Island_from_North.jpg)

Mount Lidgbird and Mount Gower viewed from Mount Eliza

Both the north and south sections of the island are the high ground of the relatively untouched forest, in the south comprising two [volcanic mountains](/wiki/Volcanic_mountains "Volcanic mountains"), [Mount Lidgbird](/wiki/Mount_Lidgbird "Mount Lidgbird") (777 m (2,549 ft)) and [Mount Gower](/wiki/Mount_Gower "Mount Gower") which, rising to 875 m (2,871 ft), is the highest point on the island.[[124]](#cite_note-124) The two mountains are separated by the [saddle](/wiki/Saddle_(landform) "Saddle (landform)") at the head of [Erskine Valley](/wiki/Erskine_Valley "Erskine Valley"). In the north, where most of the population lives, high points are Malabar (209 m (686 ft)) and Mount Eliza (147 m (482 ft)). Between these two uplands is an area of cleared lowland with some farming, an airstrip, and housing.

The Lord Howe Island Group of islands comprises 28 islands, islets, and rocks. Apart from Lord Howe Island itself, the most notable of these is the pointed rocky islet [Balls Pyramid](/wiki/Ball%27s_Pyramid "Ball's Pyramid"), a 551 m-high (1,808 ft) eroded volcano about 23 km (14 mi) to the southeast, which is uninhabited by humans but bird-colonised.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton198681–84-125) It contains the only known wild population of the [Lord Howe Island stick insect](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect "Lord Howe Island stick insect"), formerly thought to be extinct.[[126]](#cite_note-126) To the north is the [Admiralty Group](/wiki/Admiralty_Group "Admiralty Group"), a cluster of seven small, uninhabited islands.[[8]](#cite_note-Hutton81-8) Just off the east coast is 4.5 ha (11 acres) Mutton Bird Island, and in the lagoon is the 2.4 ha (5.9 acres) [Blackburn (Rabbit) Island](/wiki/Blackburn_Island "Blackburn Island").

### Geological origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Geological origins")]

Lord Howe Island is the highly eroded remains of a 7-million-year-old [shield volcano](/wiki/Shield_volcano "Shield volcano"),[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcDougallEmbletonStone1981-127) the product of eruptions that lasted for about 500,000 years.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19867-128) It is one of a chain of islands that occur on the western rim of an undersea shelf, the [Lord Howe Rise](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Rise "Lord Howe Rise"), which is 3,000 km (1,900 mi) long and 300 km (190 mi) wide continental ribbon extending from New Zealand to the west of New Caledonia and consisting of continental rocks that separated from the Australian plate 60 to 80 million years ago ([Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") to [Paleogene](/wiki/Paleogene "Paleogene")) to form a new crust in the deep [Tasman Basin](/wiki/Tasman_Basin "Tasman Basin").[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Der_Linden1969-129)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersFlaniganNebelNebel-Jacobsen20232._Geological_setting-130) The shelf is part of [Zealandia](/wiki/Zealandia "Zealandia"), a [microcontinent](/wiki/Microcontinent "Microcontinent") nearly half the size of Australia that gradually submerged after breaking away from the [Gondwanan](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") supercontinent.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallisTrewick20093548-131) The [Lord Howe Seamount Chain](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Seamount_Chain "Lord Howe Seamount Chain") is defined by coral-capped [guyots](/wiki/Guyot "Guyot") stretching to the north of the island for 1,000 km (620 mi) and including the [Middleton](/wiki/Middleton_Reef "Middleton Reef") (220 km (140 mi) away) and [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Reef "Elizabeth Reef") (160 km (99 mi) away) reefs of the [Elizabeth and Middleton Reefs Marine National Park Reserve](/wiki/Elizabeth_and_Middleton_Reefs_Marine_National_Park_Reserve "Elizabeth and Middleton Reefs Marine National Park Reserve"). This chain of nine volcanic peaks was probably produced by the northward movement of the [Australian Plate](/wiki/Australian_Plate "Australian Plate") over a stationary [hotspot](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)"), so the oldest guyots were the first formed and most northerly as the plate moved northward at a rate of 6 cm (2.4 in) per year (see [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics")).[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19867–14-132)[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton200930-133) The chain has also been called the Lord Howe volcanic complex as it has characteristics of a [monogenetic volcanic field](/wiki/Monogenetic_volcanic_field "Monogenetic volcanic field").[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamsJones2024-134)

The Lord Howe Seamount Chain is parallel to the [Tasmantid Seamount Chain](/wiki/Tasmantid_Seamount_Chain "Tasmantid Seamount Chain"). Because detailed compositional analysis elsewhere has suggested that as well as individual compositional maturation with time within an individual seamount chain, there may be underlying shared [mantle plume](/wiki/Mantle_plume "Mantle plume") sources in such parallel chains, this issue has been examined with samples from Lord Howe Island. These samples share certain compositional analysis similarities with the volcanics from [Taupo Bank](/wiki/Taupo_Bank "Taupo Bank") which would be consistent with a common or similar mantle plume source.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersFlaniganNebelNebel-Jacobsen20236._Conclusions-135) The emerging data is consistent with continental rift basalt to continental intraplate basalt origins rather than as previously believed by some [ocean island basalt](/wiki/Ocean_island_basalt "Ocean island basalt").[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamsJones2024-134)

### Basalts and calcarenite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Basalts and calcarenite")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/BasaltDykes_LordHoweIsland_7June2011.jpg/250px-BasaltDykes_LordHoweIsland_7June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:BasaltDykes_LordHoweIsland_7June2011.jpg)

Basalt [dikes](/wiki/Dike_(geology) "Dike (geology)") on the eastern cliffs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Volcanic_breccia_Mt_Lidgbird_beach_Lord_Howe_Island_10June2011.jpg/250px-Volcanic_breccia_Mt_Lidgbird_beach_Lord_Howe_Island_10June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volcanic_breccia_Mt_Lidgbird_beach_Lord_Howe_Island_10June2011.jpg)

Volcanic [breccia](/wiki/Breccia "Breccia") on Mount Lidgbird beach

Two periods of volcanic activity produced the major features of the island. The first from 7.20 ± 0.08 Ma produced the northern and central hills,[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersFlaniganNebelNebel-Jacobsen20232._Geological_setting-130) while the younger and highly eroded Mount Gower and Mount Lidgbird were produced about 6.3 Ma by successive [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") (an extrusive [igneous rock](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock")) [lava flows](/wiki/Lava_flow "Lava flow") that once filled a large volcanic [caldera](/wiki/Caldera "Caldera") (crater)[[136]](#cite_note-136) and can now be seen as horizontal basalt strata on mountain cliffs (at Malabar and Mount Gower) occasionally interspersed with [dikes](/wiki/Dike_(geology) "Dike (geology)") (vertical lava intrusions).[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEtheridge1889112–113-138) The youngest rocks are dated 4.40 ± 0.13 Ma but this recent age might be artifact.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersFlaniganNebelNebel-Jacobsen20232._Geological_setting-130) Geological [pyroclastic](/wiki/Pyroclastic_rock "Pyroclastic rock") remnants of a volcanic eruption can be seen on the 15 ha (37-acre) Roach Island (where the oldest rocks occur) and Boat Harbour as [tuff](/wiki/Tuff "Tuff") (ash), [breccia](/wiki/Breccia "Breccia") (with angular blocks), and [agglomerate](/wiki/Agglomerate "Agglomerate") (rounded 'bombs').[[15]](#cite_note-info-15) Offshore on the Lord Howe Rise, water depths reach 2,000 m (6,600 ft) falling to 4,000 m (13,000 ft) to the west of the rise. From the dimensions of the rock on which the island stands, the island has been calculated to erode to 1/40th of its original size.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuttonHarrison200411-139) Balls Pyramid composition studies suggest that its source has been more strongly influenced by metasomatism than Lord Howe Island lavas, with a discrete asthenosphere source rather than a plume.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamsJones2024-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Calcarenite_AndersonSt2_LordHoweIsland_5June2011.jpg/250px-Calcarenite_AndersonSt2_LordHoweIsland_5June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calcarenite_AndersonSt2_LordHoweIsland_5June2011.jpg)

Close-up of calcarenite by Andersons Road

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Calcarenite_karst_Lagoon_Beach_LordHoweIsland_11June2011.jpg/250px-Calcarenite_karst_Lagoon_Beach_LordHoweIsland_11June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calcarenite_karst_Lagoon_Beach_LordHoweIsland_11June2011.jpg)

Stratified calcarenite at Lagoon Beach

Rocks and land at the foot of these mountains is [calcarenite](/wiki/Calcarenite "Calcarenite"), coral sand, blown inland during the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") between 130,000 and 20,000 years ago and cemented into stratified layers by water percolation.[[140]](#cite_note-140) In this rock are fossils of bird bones and eggs, land and marine snails, and the extinct giant horned turtle (*[Meiolania](/wiki/Meiolania "Meiolania") platyceps*) which is not closely related to any living kind of turtle. The crescent of the island protects a coral reef and lagoon; the barrier reef, at 31°30'S, is the most southerly in the world.[[141]](#cite_note-Bleaching-141) Beach sands, rather than consisting of [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") grains derived from [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite"), as on the mainland, are made of fragments of [shell](/wiki/Seashell "Seashell"), coral, and [coralline algae](/wiki/Coralline_algae "Coralline algae"), together with basalt grains, and basaltic minerals such as black [diopside](/wiki/Diopside "Diopside") and green [olivine](/wiki/Olivine "Olivine").[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcDougallEmbletonStone1981-127) The lowland consists of [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial "Alluvial") soils.

The island continues to erode rapidly and is expected to be fully submerged within 200,000 years, taking an appearance akin to the Middleton and [Elizabeth Reefs](/wiki/Elizabeth_Reef "Elizabeth Reef").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGavriletsVose20072910–2921-142)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Climate")]

Lord Howe Island has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa* under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")).[[143]](#cite_note-143)

In general, the summers are warm and humid with erratic rainfall, but occasionally heavy, while in winter it is very mild with rainfall more or less uniform. There is a gradual transition from summer to winter conditions and vice versa.[[144]](#cite_note-MarineParkPlan-144) Winds are frequent and salt-laden, being moderate easterlies in the summer and fresh to strong westerlies in the winter.[[144]](#cite_note-MarineParkPlan-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) July is the windiest month,[[146]](#cite_note-146) and the winter months are subject to frequent gales and strong winds. The island has 67.8 clear days, annually.[[144]](#cite_note-MarineParkPlan-144)

Storms and occasional cyclones also affect the island.[[144]](#cite_note-MarineParkPlan-144) Rainfall records are maintained in the north, where rainfall is less than in the frequently cloud-shrouded mountains of the south. Wide variations in rainfall can occur from year to year. July and August are the coldest months with average minimum temperatures around 13 °C (55 °F) and no frost. Average maximum temperatures range from 17–20 °C (63–68 °F) in the winter to 24–27 °C (75–81 °F) in the summer. The humidity averages in the 60–70% range year-round, becoming more noticeable on warmer summer days than in the cooler winter months.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen199414–15-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)

The average temperature of the sea ranges from 20.0 °C (68.0 °F) in July, August, and September to 25.3 °C (77.5 °F) in March.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

## Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

### Plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Plants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Barringtonia_asiatica_SW_Pacific_Drift_Blinky_Beach_LordHoweIsland_9June2011.jpg/250px-Barringtonia_asiatica_SW_Pacific_Drift_Blinky_Beach_LordHoweIsland_9June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barringtonia_asiatica_SW_Pacific_Drift_Blinky_Beach_LordHoweIsland_9June2011.jpg)

Box-shaped fruit of *[Barringtonia asiatica](/wiki/Barringtonia_asiatica "Barringtonia asiatica")*, SW Pacific washed up on Blinky Beach

Lord Howe Island is a distinct terrestrial [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") known as the Lord Howe Island [subtropical forests](/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_moist_broadleaf_forests "Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests"). It is part of the [Australasian realm](/wiki/Australasian_realm "Australasian realm") and shares many biotic affinities with Australia, [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"), and [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia").[[153]](#cite_note-153) In geological terms at 7 million years old, Lord Howe Island is relatively young and was never part of any continent, its flora and fauna colonising the island from across the sea, carried by the wind, water, or birds, possibly assisted at a geological time when other islands were exposed, enabling [island hopping](/wiki/Oceanic_dispersal "Oceanic dispersal").[[154]](#cite_note-154) Nevertheless, it has been sufficiently remote for long enough to evolve [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") species.

[![Photograph of a white flower with 6 petals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Dietes_robinsoniana_qtl1.jpg/250px-Dietes_robinsoniana_qtl1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dietes_robinsoniana_qtl1.jpg)

Wedding flower (*[Dietes robinsoniana](/wiki/Dietes_robinsoniana "Dietes robinsoniana")*)

The high degree of endemism is emphasised by the presence of five endemic genera: *[Negria](/wiki/Negria "Negria")*, *[Lordhowea](/wiki/Lordhowea_(plant) "Lordhowea (plant)")*, *[Howea](/wiki/Howea "Howea")*, *[Lepidorrhachis](/wiki/Lepidorrhachis "Lepidorrhachis")*, and *[Hedyscepe](/wiki/Hedyscepe "Hedyscepe")*.[[155]](#cite_note-Green_1994_2-155) Island plants are similar to those of Norfolk Island, the two islands sharing some endemic species, for example, the critically endangered species of creeping vine *[Calystegia affinis](/wiki/Calystegia_affinis "Calystegia affinis")*. The combined flora of these two islands is more closely related to [that of New Zealand](/wiki/Flora_of_New_Zealand "Flora of New Zealand") and [New Caledonia](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_New_Caledonia#Flora "Biodiversity of New Caledonia") than to that of Australia.[[155]](#cite_note-Green_1994_2-155) Also, a small but clear link exists with the plants of [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu").[[155]](#cite_note-Green_1994_2-155) The closest mainland affinities are with the vegetation of subtropical southeastern [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"). A link with [Gondwanaland](/wiki/Gondwanaland "Gondwanaland") is indicated by the presence of endemic species such as the wedding lily (*[Dietes robinsoniana](/wiki/Dietes_robinsoniana "Dietes robinsoniana")*) whose only living relatives occur in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen19941-156)

The flora of the island is relatively untouched, with a large number of rare plants, 44% being [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") to the island.[[155]](#cite_note-Green_1994_2-155) With a diversity of conditions ranging from valleys to ridges, plains, and misty mountain tops, habitat is available for a wide range of plant communities, which have been comprehensively analysed and mapped.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPickard1983b133–236-157) Many of the island's unique plants grow on or around the mountain summits, where the height has allowed the development of a true [cloud forest](/wiki/Cloud_forest "Cloud forest") and many different microhabitats, from sea level to the summits.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton198635-158) With the increased humidity brought by the clouds on [Mount Gower](/wiki/Mount_Gower "Mount Gower") and the other mountain tops, the range of endemic plants include [mosses](/wiki/Moss "Moss"), [ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern") and flowering plants.[[159]](#cite_note-cloudforest-159)

One of the best-known plant genera endemic to Lord Howe Island is *[Howea](/wiki/Howea "Howea")*, an endemic [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") of palms ([Arecaceae](/wiki/Arecaceae "Arecaceae")) that are commonly known as [kentia palms](/wiki/Howea_forsteriana "Howea forsteriana") and are popular houseplants.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Mosses include *[Spiridens muelleri](/wiki/Spiridens_muelleri "Spiridens muelleri")*. There are 57 species of [ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern"), of which 25 are endemic: they are most abundant in the moist environments of the southern island, especially the higher parts of Mount Gower,[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton2010a4-161) perhaps the most apparent being the four endemic tree ferns in the genus *[Cyathea](/wiki/Cyathea "Cyathea")* that occur on the southern mountains.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton2010a37-162) *[Hedyscepe](/wiki/Hedyscepe "Hedyscepe")* and *[Lepidorrhachis](/wiki/Lepidorrhachis "Lepidorrhachis")* are the other two palm genera that are also endemic to the island.[[163]](#cite_note-GP2008-163)

Since the rodent eradication program, researchers have observed an increase in the amount of growth and seeds, especially of the "larger, fleshy, fruited plants", previously eaten by rats. As the [understorey](/wiki/Understorey "Understorey") grows thicker, this in turn will provide habitat for small animals such as snails and insects, which in turn provide food for the birds.[[159]](#cite_note-cloudforest-159)

#### Communities and special plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Communities and special plants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Lepidorrhachis_mooreana_LHI_fruiting.jpg/250px-Lepidorrhachis_mooreana_LHI_fruiting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lepidorrhachis_mooreana_LHI_fruiting.jpg)

Little mountain palm (*[Lepidorrhachis](/wiki/Lepidorrhachis "Lepidorrhachis") mooreana*) on the summit of Mount Gower

Plant communities have been classified into nine categories: lowland subtropical rainforest, submontane rainforest, cloud-forest and scrub, lowland swamp forest, mangrove scrub and seagrass, coastal scrub and cliff vegetation, inland scrub and herbland, offshore island vegetation, shoreline and beach vegetation, and disturbed vegetation.[[155]](#cite_note-Green_1994_2-155)
Several plants are immediately evident to the visitor. Banyan (*[Ficus macrophylla](/wiki/Ficus_macrophylla "Ficus macrophylla")* subsp. *columnaris*) is a remarkable tree with a buttressed trunk and pendulous aerial roots; it can be seen on the track to Clear Place and near Ned's Beach.[[164]](#cite_note-Plant_Life-164) Pandanus tree (*[Pandanus](/wiki/Pandanus "Pandanus") forsteri*) has spectacular teepee-like prop roots and pineapple-like fruits that are orange-red when mature, the tough leaves being used for basketry. It occurs in damp areas such as creek beds, and fine specimens can be seen along the Boat Harbour track.[[164]](#cite_note-Plant_Life-164) Ten species of orchids are on the island, the most noticeable being the bush orchid (*[Dendrobium macropus *subsp.* howeanum](/wiki/Dendrobium_gracilicaule "Dendrobium gracilicaule")*) on lowland trees and rocks, bearing cream flowers from August to September.[[164]](#cite_note-Plant_Life-164) Other prominent flowering plants in the summer include, on the mountain slopes, the whiskery red flowers of mountain rose (*[Metrosideros nervulosa](/wiki/Metrosideros_nervulosa "Metrosideros nervulosa")* and *[Metrosideros sclerocarpa](/wiki/Metrosideros_sclerocarpa "Metrosideros sclerocarpa")*), the massed, small, yellow flowers of corokia (*[Corokia carpodetoides](/wiki/Corokia_carpodetoides "Corokia carpodetoides")*), orange, plump flowers of pumpkin tree (*[Negria](/wiki/Negria "Negria") rhabdothamnoides*), and white spikes of Fitzgerald tree (*[Dracophyllum fitzgeraldii](/wiki/Dracophyllum_fitzgeraldii "Dracophyllum fitzgeraldii")*).[[164]](#cite_note-Plant_Life-164) The [kava bush](/wiki/Macropiper_hooglandii "Macropiper hooglandii") has large, aromatic, heart-shaped leaves. After heavy rain, the endemic glowing [mushroom](/wiki/Mushroom "Mushroom") *[Mycena chlorophanos](/w/index.php?title=Mycena_chlorophanos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mycena chlorophanos (page does not exist)")*, and *[Omphalotus nidiformis](/wiki/Omphalotus_nidiformis "Omphalotus nidiformis")*, can be found in the palm forests.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

The palms are the signature plants of the island as the kentia and curly palms especially dominate the landscape in many places, the kentia being of special economic importance.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200698–105-106) All four species are endemic to the island, often occurring in dense, pure stands, the one that has proved such a worldwide success as an indoor plant being the kentia or thatch palm (*Howea forsteriana*). This is a lowland palm with drooping leaflets and seed branches in 'hands' of three to five, while the curly palm (*H. belmoreana*), which occurs on slightly higher ground, has upwardly directed leaflets and solitary 'hands'. Natural hybrids between these species occur on the island and a mature specimen of one is growing in the island nursery. On the mountain sides higher than about 350 m, the big mountain palm (*[Hedyscepe](/wiki/Hedyscepe "Hedyscepe") canterburyana*) occurs; it has large, golf ball-sized fruits, while the little mountain palm (*[Lepidorrhachis](/wiki/Lepidorrhachis "Lepidorrhachis") mooreana*) has marble-sized fruits and is only found on the mountain summits.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols200698-166)

### Animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Animals")]

No snakes nor poisonous or venomous animals or plants occur, and no dangerous daytime sharks are found off the beaches, although tiger sharks have been reported on the cliff side of the island.[[167]](#cite_note-Hutt1-167)

#### Birds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Birds")]

[![Close-up picture of the sooty tern](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Sterna_fuscata.JPG/250px-Sterna_fuscata.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sterna_fuscata.JPG)

Sooty terns are the most abundant breeding seabirds and are common along the east coast.

A total of 202 different birds have been recorded on the island. Eighteen species of land birds breed on the island and many more [migratory](/wiki/Bird_migration "Bird migration") species occur on the island and its adjacent islets, many tame enough that humans can get quite close.[[168]](#cite_note-Hutt-168) The island has been identified by [BirdLife International](/wiki/BirdLife_International "BirdLife International") as an [endemic bird area](/wiki/Endemic_bird_area "Endemic bird area"), and the Permanent Park Preserve as an [important bird area](/wiki/Important_bird_area "Important bird area") because it supports the entire population of [Lord Howe woodhens](/wiki/Lord_Howe_woodhen "Lord Howe woodhen"), most of the breeding population of [providence petrels](/wiki/Providence_petrel "Providence petrel"), over 1% of the world population of another five seabird species, and the whole populations of three endemic subspecies.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

Fourteen species of [seabirds](/wiki/Seabird "Seabird") breed on the island.[[168]](#cite_note-Hutt-168) [Red-tailed tropicbirds](/wiki/Red-tailed_tropicbird "Red-tailed tropicbird") can be seen in large numbers circling the Malabar cliffs, where they perform acrobatic courting rituals.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199084-170) Between August and May, thousands of [flesh-footed](/wiki/Flesh-footed_shearwater "Flesh-footed shearwater") and [wedge-tailed shearwaters](/wiki/Wedge-tailed_shearwater "Wedge-tailed shearwater") return to the island at dusk each day. From the Little Island Track between March and November, one of the world's rarest birds, the providence petrel, also performs courtship displays during winter breeding, and it is extremely tame. The island was its only breeding location for many years after the breeding colony on Norfolk Island was exterminated in the late 19th century,[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199068-171) though a small population persists on the adjacent [Phillip Island](/wiki/Phillip_Island_(Norfolk_Island) "Phillip Island (Norfolk Island)"). The [Kermadec petrel](/wiki/Kermadec_petrel "Kermadec petrel") was discovered breeding on Mount Gower in 1914 by ornithologist [Roy Bell](/wiki/Roy_Bell_(ornithologist) "Roy Bell (ornithologist)") while collecting specimens for [Gregory Mathews](/wiki/Gregory_Mathews "Gregory Mathews")[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199070-172) and the [black-winged petrel](/wiki/Black-winged_petrel "Black-winged petrel") was only confirmed as a breeder in 1971; its numbers have increased following the elimination of [feral cats](/wiki/Feral_cat "Feral cat") from the island.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199072-173)

[![Photograph of a black bird on a beach](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Black_Noddy_Anous_minutus_North_Beach_LordHoweIsland_6June2011.jpg/250px-Black_Noddy_Anous_minutus_North_Beach_LordHoweIsland_6June2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Noddy_Anous_minutus_North_Beach_LordHoweIsland_6June2011.jpg)

Black noddy on North Beach

The flesh-footed shearwater, which breeds in large numbers on the main island in spring-autumn, once had its [chicks harvested for food](/wiki/Muttonbirding "Muttonbirding") by the islanders.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199074-174) The wedge-tailed and [little shearwaters](/wiki/Little_shearwater "Little shearwater") breed on the main island and surrounding islets, though only a small number of the latter species can be found on the main island. Breeding [white-bellied storm petrels](/wiki/White-bellied_storm_petrel "White-bellied storm petrel") was another discovery by Roy Bell.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199080-175) [Masked boobies](/wiki/Masked_boobies "Masked boobies") are the largest seabirds breeding on Lord Howe[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199082-176) and can be seen nesting and gliding along the sea cliffs at Mutton Bird Point all year round. [Sooty terns](/wiki/Sooty_tern "Sooty tern") can be seen on the main island at Ned's and Middle Beaches, North Bay, and Blinkey Beach; the most numerous of the island's breeding seabirds, their eggs were formerly harvested for food. [Common](/wiki/Common_noddy "Common noddy") and [black noddies](/wiki/Black_noddy "Black noddy") build nests in trees and bushes, while [white terns](/wiki/White_tern "White tern") lay their single eggs precariously in a slight depression on a tree branch,[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199092-177) and [grey ternlets](/wiki/Grey_noddy "Grey noddy") lay their eggs in cliff hollows.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199092–94-178)

Three endemic [passerine](/wiki/Passerine "Passerine") [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies "Subspecies") are the [Lord Howe golden whistler](/wiki/Lord_Howe_golden_whistler "Lord Howe golden whistler"), [Lord Howe silvereye](/wiki/Lord_Howe_silvereye "Lord Howe silvereye"), and [Lord Howe currawong](/wiki/Lord_Howe_currawong "Lord Howe currawong").[[179]](#cite_note-Hutton_1990-179) The iconic endemic rail, the flightless [Lord Howe woodhen](/wiki/Lord_Howe_woodhen "Lord Howe woodhen"), is the only surviving member of its genus; its ancestors could fly, but with no predators and plenty of food on the island, this ability was lost. This made it easy prey for islanders and feral animals, so by the 1970s, the population was less than 30 birds. From 1978 to 1984, feral animals were removed and birds were raised in captivity to be successfully reintroduced to the wild. The population is now relatively safe and stable.[[15]](#cite_note-info-15)[[180]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_121-180)

[Domestic Mallards](/wiki/Domestic_duck "Domestic duck") have colonised Lord Howe Island from New Zealand. They have replaced the [Pacific Black Duck](/wiki/Pacific_black_duck "Pacific black duck") through competition and [introgressive hybridisation](/wiki/Introgression "Introgression").
