#### Mammals, reptiles and amphibians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Mammals, reptiles and amphibians")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Meiolania_Platyceps%2C_Lord_Howe_Island_Maritime_Museum.jpg/250px-Meiolania_Platyceps%2C_Lord_Howe_Island_Maritime_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meiolania_Platyceps,_Lord_Howe_Island_Maritime_Museum.jpg)

Skeleton of the horned turtle *[Meiolania platyceps](/wiki/Meiolania "Meiolania")*, once native to Lorde Howe

Only one native [mammal](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") remains on the islands, the [large forest bat](/wiki/Large_forest_bat "Large forest bat"). The endemic [Lord Howe long-eared bat](/wiki/Lord_Howe_long-eared_bat "Lord Howe long-eared bat") is known only from a skull and is now presumed extinct, possibly the result of the introduction of [ship rats](/wiki/Ship_rat "Ship rat").[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDanielWilliams1984-181)

Two terrestrial reptiles are native to the island group: the [Lord Howe Island skink](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_skink "Lord Howe Island skink") and the [Lord Howe Island gecko](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_gecko "Lord Howe Island gecko"). Both are rare on the main island, but more common on smaller islands offshore.[[182]](#cite_note-RecherSurvey-182) The [garden skink](/wiki/Lampropholis_delicata "Lampropholis delicata") and the [bleating tree frog](/wiki/Bleating_tree_frog "Bleating tree frog") have been accidentally introduced from the Australian mainland. During the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") the giant terrestrial horned [turtle](/wiki/Turtle "Turtle") *[Meiolania](/wiki/Meiolania "Meiolania") platyceps* was endemic to the island, but this is currently thought to have gone extinct before human occupation as a result of postglacial sea-level rise.

#### Invertebrates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Invertebrates")]

[![Picture of the rare dark brown Lord Howe Island stick insect on, and about the same length as, a person's hand](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect_Dryococelus_australis_10June2011_PalmNursery.jpg/250px-Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect_Dryococelus_australis_10June2011_PalmNursery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect_Dryococelus_australis_10June2011_PalmNursery.jpg)

[Lord Howe Island stick insect](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_stick_insect "Lord Howe Island stick insect")

The [Lord Howe stick insect](/wiki/Dryococelus "Dryococelus") disappeared from the main island soon after the accidental introduction of rats when the SS *Makambo* ran aground near Ned's Beach on 15 June 1918.[[182]](#cite_note-RecherSurvey-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183) In 2001, a tiny population was discovered in a single *[Melaleuca howeana](/wiki/Melaleuca_howeana "Melaleuca howeana")* shrub on the slopes of Ball's Pyramid,[[184]](#cite_note-184) has been successfully bred in captivity, and is nearing re-introduction to the main island.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[185]](#cite_note-185) The [Lord Howe stag beetle](/wiki/Lord_Howe_stag_beetle "Lord Howe stag beetle") is a colourful endemic beetle seen during summers.[[186]](#cite_note-186) Another endemic invertebrate, the [Lord Howe flax snail](/wiki/Lord_Howe_flax_snail "Lord Howe flax snail") (or Lord Howe *Placostylus*), has also been affected by the introduction of rats.[[182]](#cite_note-RecherSurvey-182) Once common, the species is now endangered and a captive-breeding program is underway to save the snail from extinction. An [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") species that is likely to have become extinct due to human-introduced rats was the large [rove beetle](/wiki/Rove_beetle "Rove beetle") *[Hesperus gigas](/w/index.php?title=Hesperus_gigas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hesperus gigas (page does not exist)")* (Lea, 1929) comb. nov. (formerly *Cafius gigas* Lea, 1929) (Coleoptera: Staphylinidae).[[187]](#cite_note-187) The Lord Howe Island wood-feeding cockroach (*[Panesthia lata](/wiki/Panesthia_lata "Panesthia lata")*) was thought extirpated from the island until a small population was found.

#### Marine life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Marine life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Surge_wrasse_Lorde_Howe_Island.jpg/250px-Surge_wrasse_Lorde_Howe_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surge_wrasse_Lorde_Howe_Island.jpg)

[Surge wrasse](/wiki/Surge_wrasse "Surge wrasse") swimming in the waters of Ned's Beach

Marine environments are near-pristine with a mixture of temperate, subtropical, and tropical species derived from cool-temperate ocean currents in the winter and the warm [East Australian Current](/wiki/East_Australian_Current "East Australian Current"), which flows from the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), in summer.[[188]](#cite_note-Huttharr-188) Of the 490 fish species recorded, 13 are endemic and 60% are tropical.[[188]](#cite_note-Huttharr-188) The main angling fish are yellowtail kingfish and [New Zealand bluefish](/wiki/New_Zealand_bluefish "New Zealand bluefish"), while game fish include marlin, tuna, and giant kingfish called "greenbacks".[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton198673-189) Over 80 species of corals occur in the reefs surrounding the islands.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVeronDone1979-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191) Australian underwater photographer [Neville Coleman](/wiki/Neville_Coleman "Neville Coleman") has photographed various [nudibranchs](/wiki/Nudibranch "Nudibranch") at Lord Howe Island.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

Various species of [cetaceans](/wiki/Cetacean "Cetacean") inhabit or migrate through the waters in the vicinity, but very little about their biology in the area is known due to a lack of studies and sighting efforts caused by locational conditions. [Bottlenose dolphins](/wiki/Bottlenose_dolphin "Bottlenose dolphin") are the most commonly observed and are the only species confirmed to be seasonal or yearly residents, while some other [dolphin](/wiki/Dolphin "Dolphin") species have also been observed.[[193]](#cite_note-193) [Humpback whales](/wiki/Humpback_whale "Humpback whale") are the only large whales showing slow but steady recoveries as their numbers annually migrating past the island of Lord Howe are much smaller than those migrating along the Australian continent.

Historically, migratory whales such as [blue](/wiki/Blue_whale "Blue whale"), [fin](/wiki/Fin_whale "Fin whale"), and [sei whales](/wiki/Sei_whale "Sei whale") were very abundant in the island waters but were severely reduced in numbers to near-extinction by commercial and illegal hunts, including the mass illegal hunts by the Soviet Union and Japan in the 1960s to 1970s.[[194]](#cite_note-194) [Southern right](/wiki/Southern_right_whale "Southern right whale") and [sperm whales](/wiki/Sperm_whale "Sperm whale") were most severely hunted among these, hence the area was called the Middle Ground by whalers.[[195]](#cite_note-Nic2006-195) These two were likely once seasonal residents around the island, where right whales prefer sheltered, very shallow bays,[[196]](#cite_note-196) while sperm whales mainly inhabit deep waters.

### Conservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Conservation")]

[![Old coloured painting by George Raper in 1790 depicting the now extinct Lord Howe white-throated pigeon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/LordHoweIslandPigeonByGeorgeRaper.jpg/250px-LordHoweIslandPigeonByGeorgeRaper.jpg)](/wiki/File:LordHoweIslandPigeonByGeorgeRaper.jpg)

Lord Howe white-throated pigeon (*[Columba vitiensis godmanae](/wiki/Columba_vitiensis_godmanae "Columba vitiensis godmanae")*) painted by [George Raper](/wiki/George_Raper "George Raper"), 1790 – now extinct

About 10% of Lord Howe Island's forests have been cleared for agriculture, and another 20% has been disturbed, mostly by domestic cattle and feral sheep, goats, and pigs. As a result, 70% of the island remains relatively untouched, with a variety of plants and animals, many of which are endemic, and some of which are rare or threatened.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Two species of plants, nine terrestrial birds, one bat, and at least four invertebrates have become extinct since 1778.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuttonParkesSinclair200722-198) Endemism at the [generic](/wiki/Genus "Genus") level includes the palms *[Howea](/wiki/Howea "Howea")*, *[Hedyscepe](/wiki/Hedyscepe "Hedyscepe")* and *[Lepidorrhachis](/wiki/Lepidorrhachis "Lepidorrhachis")*, a woody daisy *[Lordhowea insularis](/wiki/Lordhowea_insularis "Lordhowea insularis")*, the tree *[Negria](/wiki/Negria "Negria")*, the leech *[Quantenobdella howensis](/w/index.php?title=Quantenobdella_howensis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Quantenobdella howensis (page does not exist)")*, three annelid worm genera (*[Paraplutellus](/w/index.php?title=Paraplutellus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paraplutellus (page does not exist)")*, *[Pericryptodrilus](/w/index.php?title=Pericryptodrilus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pericryptodrilus (page does not exist)")* and *[Eastoniella](/w/index.php?title=Eastoniella&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eastoniella (page does not exist)")*), an [isopod](/wiki/Isopod "Isopod") shrimp *[Stigmops](/w/index.php?title=Stigmops&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stigmops (page does not exist)")*, a [hemipteran](/wiki/Hemipteran "Hemipteran") bug *[Howeria](/w/index.php?title=Howeria&action=edit&redlink=1 "Howeria (page does not exist)")*, and a cricket *[Howeta](/w/index.php?title=Howeta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Howeta (page does not exist)")*.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuttonParkesSinclair200724-199)

The Lord Howe Island Board instigated an extensive biological and [environmental survey](/wiki/Environmental_surveying "Environmental surveying") (published in 1974), which has guided the island conservation program.[[200]](#cite_note-200) A group of scientists including director of the [Australian Museum](/wiki/Australian_Museum "Australian Museum"), [Frank Talbot](/wiki/Frank_Talbot "Frank Talbot"), and others from the [Royal Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Garden,_Sydney "Royal Botanic Garden, Sydney"), the [National Parks and Wildlife Service](/wiki/National_Parks_and_Wildlife_Service_(New_South_Wales) "National Parks and Wildlife Service (New South Wales)"), and the [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO") undertook an environmental survey of the island in 1970, reporting on environmental degradation there.[[201]](#cite_note-201)

In 1981, the Lord Howe Island Amendment Act proclaimed a "Permanent Park Preserve" over the north and south ends of the island. Administration of the preserve was outlined in a management plan for the sustainable development of the island prepared by the NSW [National Parks and Wildlife Service](/wiki/National_Parks_and_Wildlife_Service_(New_South_Wales) "National Parks and Wildlife Service (New South Wales)"),[[110]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_117-110)[[202]](#cite_note-202) which has a ranger stationed on the island. The island was cited under the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1982.

Offshore environmental assets are protected by the [Lord Howe Island Marine Park](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Marine_Park "Lord Howe Island Marine Park"). This consists of a state marine park managed by the Marine Parks Authority of New South Wales in the waters out to three nautical miles around the island and including Ball's Pyramid.[[20]](#cite_note-MarinePark-20) It also includes a [Commonwealth Marine Park](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island_Marine_Park_(Commonwealth_waters) "Lord Howe Island Marine Park (Commonwealth waters)") extending from 3 to 12 nautical miles out and managed by the federal [Department of the Environment and Heritage](/wiki/Department_of_Sustainability,_Environment,_Water,_Population_and_Communities "Department of Sustainability, Environment, Water, Population and Communities").[[203]](#cite_note-203) In total the Marine Park covers about 3,005 km2 (1,160 sq mi).[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuttonHarrison20046-204)

#### Feral animals and plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Feral animals and plants")]

Pigs and goats were released on the island as potential food sources in the early 1800s; the goats destroyed shrubs and grasses used as nesting sites and the pigs ate eggs and chicks and disturbed the land by rooting for food.[[205]](#cite_note-Nich4-205) Several birds have become extinct on the island since the arrival of humans. The first round of extinctions included the [Lord Howe swamphen](/wiki/Lord_Howe_swamphen "Lord Howe swamphen") or white gallinule, the [white-throated pigeon](/wiki/White-throated_pigeon "White-throated pigeon"), the [red-crowned parakeet](/wiki/Red-crowned_parakeet "Red-crowned parakeet"), and the [Tasman booby](/wiki/Tasman_booby "Tasman booby"), which were eliminated by visitors and settlers during the 19th century, either from overhunting for food or protection of crops.[[205]](#cite_note-Nich4-205) Black rats were released from provisioning whaling ships in the 1840s and mice from Norfolk Island in 1860.[[205]](#cite_note-Nich4-205) In 1918, the [black rat](/wiki/Black_rat "Black rat") was accidentally introduced with the shipwreck of the S.S. *Makambo*, which ran aground at Ned's Beach. This triggered a second wave of extinctions, including the [vinous-tinted thrush](/wiki/Vinous-tinted_thrush "Vinous-tinted thrush"), the [robust white-eye](/wiki/Robust_white-eye "Robust white-eye"), the [Lord Howe starling](/wiki/Lord_Howe_starling "Lord Howe starling"), the [Lord Howe fantail](/wiki/Lord_Howe_fantail "Lord Howe fantail") and the [Lord Howe gerygone](/wiki/Lord_Howe_gerygone "Lord Howe gerygone"), as well as the destruction of the native phasmid and the decimation of palm fruits.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006118–119-206) Bounties were offered for rats and pig tails and 'ratting' became a popular pursuit. Subsequent poisoning programs have kept populations low.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENichols2006119-207) The [Lord Howe boobook](/wiki/Lord_Howe_boobook "Lord Howe boobook") may have become extinct through predation by, or competition with, the [Tasmanian masked owl](/wiki/Tasmanian_masked_owl "Tasmanian masked owl"), which was introduced in the 1920s in a failed attempt to control the rat population.[[205]](#cite_note-Nich4-205) Stray dogs are also a threat, as they could harm the native woodhen and other birds.[[208]](#cite_note-Woody-208)

Invasive plants such as [Crofton weed](/wiki/Crofton_weed "Crofton weed")[[209]](#cite_note-209) and [Formosa lily](/wiki/Formosa_lily "Formosa lily")[[210]](#cite_note-210) occur in inaccessible areas and probably cannot be eradicated, but others are currently being managed.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton198640-211) In 1995, the first action was taken to control the spread of introduced plants on the island, chiefly [ground asparagus](/wiki/Asparagus_aethiopicus "Asparagus aethiopicus") and [bridal creeper](/wiki/Bridal_creeper "Bridal creeper"), but also [cherry guava](/wiki/Cherry_guava "Cherry guava"), [Madeira vine](/wiki/Madeira_vine "Madeira vine"), *[Cotoneaster](/wiki/Cotoneaster "Cotoneaster")*, *[Ochna](/wiki/Ochna "Ochna")*, and *[Cestrum](/wiki/Cestrum "Cestrum")*. This has been followed by weeding tours and the formation of the Friends of Lord Howe Island group in 2000. Programs have also been started to remove weeds from private properties and re-vegetate some formerly cultivated areas.[[212]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_122–123-212) An environmental unit was created by the board and it includes a flora management officer and a permanent weed officer. Weeds have been mapped and an eradication program is in place, supported by improved education and quarantine procedures.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton2010c5-213)

Introduced species that harmed Lord Howe's native flora and fauna, namely feral pigs, cats, and goats, were eradicated by the early 2000s.[[180]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_121-180)[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[212]](#cite_note-Nichols_2006_122–123-212)[[215]](#cite_note-215)

In July 2012, the Australian federal Environment Minister [Tony Burke](/wiki/Tony_Burke "Tony Burke") and the New South Wales Environment Minister [Robyn Parker](/wiki/Robyn_Parker "Robyn Parker") announced that the Australian and New South Wales governments would each contribute 50% of the estimated A$9 million cost of implementing a rodent eradication plan for the island, using the aerial deployment of poison baits.[[216]](#cite_note-216) The plan was put to a local vote and is considered controversial.[[217]](#cite_note-Slezak8Feb-217) Around 230 woodhens were captured before the rodent eradication commenced in early 2019. Following the successful eradication of the rodents, all woodhens and currawongs were released across the island in late 2019 and early 2020.[[218]](#cite_note-218)

In 2023 the island was declared rodent free, which is a globally significant conservation milestone.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

A recovery program has restored the [Lord Howe woodhen](/wiki/Lord_Howe_woodhen "Lord Howe woodhen")'s numbers from only 20 in 1970 to about 250 birds four years before the rodent baiting program. There were 1,100 in the 2023 survey.[[208]](#cite_note-Woody-208)[[220]](#cite_note-220)

#### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Climate change")]

According to an analysis by eminent Australian academic [Tim Flannery](/wiki/Tim_Flannery "Tim Flannery"), the ecosystem of Lord Howe Island is threatened by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"), with the reefs at risk from rises in water temperature.[[141]](#cite_note-Bleaching-141)[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222) The first international conference on global [artificial photosynthesis](/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis "Artificial photosynthesis") as a climate-change solution occurred at Lord Howe Island in 2011,[[223]](#cite_note-223) the papers being published by the *Australian Journal of Chemistry*.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

## Heritage listings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Howe_Island&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Heritage listings")]

The Lord Howe Islands Group was inscribed on the World Heritage List for its unique landforms and biota, its diverse and largely intact ecosystems, its natural beauty, and its habitats for threatened species. It also has significant cultural heritage associations in the history of NSW.[[19]](#cite_note-nswshr-970-19)

Lord Howe Island and adjacent islets, Admiralty Islands, Mutton Bird Islands, Ball's Pyramid, and associated coral reefs and marine environs were added to the [Australian National Heritage List](/wiki/Australian_National_Heritage_List "Australian National Heritage List") on 21 May 2007, based on the World Heritage List.[[18]](#cite_note-ANHL-18)

Lord Howe Island was listed on the [New South Wales State Heritage Register](/wiki/New_South_Wales_State_Heritage_Register "New South Wales State Heritage Register") on 2 April 1999.[[19]](#cite_note-nswshr-970-19)

In September 2019 it was revealed that, in 2017, federal environment minister [Josh Frydenberg](/wiki/Josh_Frydenberg "Josh Frydenberg") overruled a recommendation from his department to install two wind turbines. The project, which would have substantially reduced the island's dependence on diesel-powered electricity generators, had been considered not to endanger the island's heritage status and was supported by the islanders.[[225]](#cite_note-225)
